---
title: "Titanic Passenger Survival"
---

Passenger-level data on the 1912 Titanic disaster, including age, sex, ticket class, and survival outcome. Widely used to introduce logistic regression, classification, and discussions of confounding.


## Source and Citation

- **Citation**: Kaggle Titanic dataset, derived from Encyclopedia Titanica
- **URL**: [https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic](https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic)
- **License**: CC0-1.0

## Dataset Characteristics

- **Observations**: 891
- **Variables**: 12
- **Missingness present**: TRUE
- **Longitudinal**: FALSE
- **Hierarchical**: FALSE

## Variable Dictionary

| Variable | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| PassengerId | continuous | Unique passenger identifier |
| Survived | binary | 0 = died, 1 = survived |
| Pclass | ordinal | Ticket class: 1 = first, 2 = second, 3 = third |
| Name | text | Passenger name |
| Sex | categorical | Passenger sex |
| Age | continuous | Age in years (some values missing) |
| SibSp | count | Number of siblings/spouses aboard |
| Parch | count | Number of parents/children aboard |
| Ticket | categorical | Ticket number |
| Fare | continuous | Passenger fare |
| Cabin | categorical | Cabin number (mostly missing) |
| Embarked | categorical | Port of embarkation: C, Q, or S |

## Suggested Learning Objectives

- Fit and interpret a logistic regression model
- Recognize confounding by passenger class and sex

## Suggested Classroom Activities

- Compare crude vs. adjusted survival odds by sex

## Common Student Misconceptions

- Treating fare as a clean linear predictor without checking skew

## Load This Dataset in R

```r
library(statsdata)
data <- load_dataset("titanic-survival")
```
